In this case `"react2angular/index"` is the same as `"react2angular", since the package's `package.json` defines `main` as `index.js`:
https://github.com/coatue-oss/react2angular/blob/d0b8c336b2a52e05839636b7651dad959be76e7a/package.json#L5
...which makes the `/index` redundant.

However looking at the package there is also a newer ES2015 (aka ES6) version of the file, which we can use instead:
https://unpkg.com/react2angular@3.2.1/index.es2015.js

(compare to https://unpkg.com/react2angular@3.2.1/index.js which has been generated for older browsers)

As such, I think we should use (have fixed the quotes too):
`import { react2angular } from 'react2angular/index.es2015';`

Maybe think of it from the other side. Say you run TaskX(), which uses dynamic dependencies. You need `new_deps` because after each `yield`-from-a-`run()`, you will add a few more dependencies. However, for each `yield`-from-a-`run()` you don't update the retry-policy of the already added task TaskX(). Sure, the new deps laso have their own retry-policies but they are added in *their* `_add_task` call. Makes sense?

Yes, I know. `parent_tracing` is tracked by context. It is part of the state that it tracks per 'async thread'. I'm assuming `Context` is the class that will hold all this state. Right now, you'll do 

    with Context(parent_tracing):
      # ...

But if we decide to add another piece of state to be tracked, you'd basically change it to

    with Context(parent_tracing, second_state):
        # ...

( _OPT_SGD, _OPT_MOMENTUM, _OPT_NAG, _OPT_ADAGRAD,

All-uppercase because they are constants, `_OPT` prefix to namespace them a little. Alternatively,

class _Optimizer:

if self.opt_type == Momentum:

I think that's indicating that the test was skipped. When you mark something `xfail`, it actually *runs* the test and ignores the result if it fails (or if you make it a strict xfail, actually fails the test suite if the test passes). `skip` doesn't run the test at all, so I believe that message is expected.

You are right that we should be switching many `xfail`s over to `skip`. I was planning on doing that at some point, but it takes time to figure out which ones are intended as an actual `xfail` (which generally means "this should work but it's a known bug - when this test starts passing drop this decorator") and which are just using the wrong decorator for `skip`.

In almost all cases, line continuations with backslashes should be avoided. Use parentheses:

```python
from pulp_smash.constants import (
...
)
```

One reason that backslashes are avoided is that comments can't be placed after them:

```
from pulp_smash.constants import FOO, \  # my comment
    BAR
```

The compiler reads this as:

```
from pulp_smash.constants import FOO, \  # my comment BAR
```

Depending on the results you probably want the second argument to is_instance to be either `six.binary_type` (str on py2, bytes on py3), `six.string_types` (str or unicode on py2, str on py3), or `(six.binary_type, six.text_type)` (str or unicode on py2, bytes or str on py3).

If we do this then it should be `if False: ...` instead of defining a variable `TESTING`. (Remember: every variable we define in this file becomes part of trio's public API!)

I *think* we probably don't need this? From some playing around, it looks like `jedi` is happy to suggest `testing` as a completion even if we don't mention it here:

```python
In [15]: "testing" in [v.name for v in jedi.Script("import trio; trio.").completions()]
Out[15]: True
```

And if I put this into a file and run `pylint` on it, then it doesn't warn on the mention of `trio.testing`, but does warn on the mention of `trio.testingxxx`, so it seems that pylint has also figured out that `trio.testing` is a valid thing:

```python
import trio
print(trio.testing)
print(trio.testingxxx)
```

(These are both with trio v0.6.0, since I happened to have it lying around.)

So there's some puzzle about how pylint actually decides what names are in a namespace: it knows about `testing`, but somehow the way we're querying it currently doesn't...

For now please either make this `if False: ...` or else special-case the name `testing` in our pylint test, and we should look to see if we can figure out pylint's API better...
